text: Hi, welcome to This is My Architecture. I am Ehsan and today I'm joined with Saras from
Alph Education. Hi Saras. Hi Ehsan, thank you for having me today. Thanks for your time. So tell us
more about Alph Education before we start looking at your architecture please. Okay, Alph Education
is an e-learning platform that want to revolutionize education through digital transformation.
Amazing. So I think you're doing a lot of great stuff when applying learning to students. So tell us
more about the architecture, how you do that. Okay, Alph Education is a one-shot platform for students,
teachers and authors to work together to build a personalized education for K-12 students.
We start with collecting information about students who are learning in the platform. There are about
120k students learning through the platform as of now and we are collecting events that compose
learning on their performance in the platform as well as how they are scoring in their assessments.
How many data points that you collect here and you put MS3? We are collecting about 100 million data
points that are active for our learning experience. Amazing. And? And we are also collecting information
about how authors are publishing this content in Alph through an authoring event and this contains
about 65k data points that are inserted into the Redshift cluster. Okay, so you have learning events
from students and then authoring events from a tour of Redshift. Exactly. Okay. Then we are also using a
Spark streaming job to transform this unstructured data that we have in S3 into Redshift and in Redshift is a
scalable data warehouse that allows us to merge both unstructured and structured data and we have over
three terabytes of data collected for all the students who are learning in the platform. So all the data will be in
scalable data warehouse. Exactly. And it will be ready for the next processing step. Exactly. What you do next?
Because we have consolidated all the data that is done by the students, teachers and authors
We are able to leverage the data that is there in the Redshift to build a personalized recommendation system for students to learn.
Okay, and that's what would lead you to the next part of the architecture. Exactly.
We are collecting this data for training this into a machine learning model that has passed to an EKS cluster.
This EKS cluster is also running on top of Kubeflow, which is an open source implementation for training machine learning models.
So you train the model with an EKS, but how do you make sure that this is the right model and the best model that you need to develop?
That's an interesting question. So we are using identifying different parameters that we can optimize for training the models.

And once we are fine tuning it, then we are passing this to SageMaker hyperparameter tuning job, where we are using different objective metrics to optimize the model and select the best model out of it.
Can you give me some examples of these metrics?
We are using a one versus all model for classifying this into objectivizing the parameters.
We are using F1 score as our primary validation metric and then we are also using recall
as a secondary objective so that we are selecting the best model with avoiding the false positive read.
Perfect. So all the hyperparameter tuning is happening here.
Exactly.
And then once you are happy with the model, what do you do next?
So SageMaker allows us to provide the best model through parameter selection and then the best model is bundled
into a docker build and published to an ACR repository.
So the ACR will be the repository for the docker as a container registry managed by the docker?
Yes.
Okay. Awesome.
So once we bundle this model, then we allow the students to use this model and we publish to an inferencing cluster in EKS.
Yes. So as of now, we have all the data coming all the way through the SageMaker into ACR.
ECR.
This is the training part.
Exactly.
How do you leverage this recommendation engine from the student's perspective?
Because we have selected the best model that is saved in ECR, it gives us an opportunity to publish this model into an EKS cluster,
where students would be exposed to this machine learning model for personalized recommendation.
And how many events do you have per day, maybe during the learning process?
We are also using about 65k events per day that are collected for recommendation.
And this is actually auto scaled based on how the students are using this in their classroom.
And when the cluster is not used, as in the students are going offline, then this cluster is auto scaled down.
So there will be some peak times and some...
Some down.
Okay, amazing. And the auto scaling will scale up and down accordingly.
Exactly.
This is again passed to the inference cluster.
Okay, so now we close the cycle of training and inferences.
But I can see CloudWatch here.
What is the role that it plays with this machine learning process?
CloudWatch provides us an important role in monitoring how we are doing our training and inference.
So we are using logs from the training cluster to collect metrics about how we should be training and optimizing the model.
For instance, if our model is diverting from what we are collecting in the form of data,
it gives us an option to use the early stopping to stop and retrain the model based on other objective functions.
And this is for training part. And what does it do with the inference part?
We are also collecting the inference data for all the machine learning models exposed
and using this to track how the students are performing when the model is published in the production.
And we are using the model objective to monitor the accuracy.
If the accuracy falls behind, we are using model lineage techniques.
And there are CloudWatch alerts that are notifying about the state of the model.
Okay, awesome.
That's great to see how you apply machine learning for recommendation engine to help the students' learning experience.
Thank you.
Amazing.
Thanks for your time, Sarah.
And thanks everyone for joining this episode of This is My Architecture.
